cover.body_html:
50亿美元。

这是AMD有史以来最大手笔的单笔投资。7月23日旧金山，AMD Advancing AI大会现场座无虚席，CEO苏姿丰（Lisa Su）站在台上亮出了两张牌：一张是代号Helios的整机柜级AI系统，单柜集成72颗Instinct MI455X加速卡、256核第六代EPYC Venice CPU，总算力达到2.9 Exaflops，多项指标直接对标NVIDIA的Vera Rubin；另一张是向Anthropic投资50亿美元，同时签署一份为期多年、总价值数百亿美元的芯片供应协议——Anthropic将从2027年上半年开始，为其数据中心采购最多2吉瓦（2GW）的AMD Instinct MI450系列GPU。

这不是一次普通的产品发布。这是AMD对NVIDIA在AI算力市场垄断地位发起的总攻。当Lisa Su说出"我们预计到2030年，AI加速器市场规模将达到约1.4万亿美元，这意味着到这个十年末，AI加速器市场将接近今天整个半导体市场的规模"时，你能清楚地感受到：AMD不想再做老二了。

Helios是什么？一台整机柜就是一个超级计算机

要理解Helios的意义，你得先理解AI算力市场正在发生什么变化。

过去几年，AI公司买GPU是"按卡买"——买几千张H100、几万张H200，自己组装服务器、自己搭网络、自己调软件栈。但随着模型越来越大，集群规模从几千卡膨胀到几万卡甚至几十万卡，"按卡买"的模式已经走不通了。你买得到GPU，不代表你能把它们高效地连在一起跑大模型。网络延迟、散热、供电、软件栈优化、故障排查——任何一个环节掉链子，整个集群的实际利用率可能连50%都不到。

于是NVIDIA率先推出了整机柜方案：从Grace Blackwell到Vera Rubin，把GPU、CPU、DPU、网络、散热、供电全部打包成一个即插即用的"超级单元"，客户买回去直接上电就能用。这一招很狠——它把竞争维度从"谁的GPU芯片快"提升到了"谁能提供开箱即用的完整算力系统"。NVIDIA凭借这一策略，在过去两年拿下了AI训练和推理市场超过80%的份额。

AMD的Helios，就是对这一策略的正面回应。

Helios的规格相当激进：单柜集成72颗Instinct MI455X GPU加速卡，搭配256核第六代EPYC Venice CPU和Pensando Vulcano DPU，采用全液冷散热，单柜总算力达到2.9 Exaflops。The Register的对比测试显示，Helios在多项关键指标上已经追平甚至超过了NVIDIA的Vera Rubin系统。这不是PPT概念——Helios已经有了一串星光熠熠的客户名单：OpenAI、Meta、Microsoft、Oracle、Anthropic，全部计划部署Helios系统。

微软CEO Satya Nadella在大会前一天就公开表态，将在Azure数据中心大规模部署Helios系统用于前沿模型推理和Azure AI服务。这是一个标志性事件：过去微软几乎是NVIDIA的铁杆盟友，Azure的AI算力几乎清一色NVIDIA GPU。现在微软公开拥抱AMD，释放的信号再明确不过——没有人愿意被一家供应商卡脖子。

"当你让Agent做一件事的时候，它实际上要执行几十个步骤——推理、调用工具、访问数据、反复迭代直到解决问题。所有这些都需要大量GPU。"—— AMD CEO 苏姿丰

50亿美元投资Anthropic：不是卖芯片，是绑战船

如果说Helios是AMD的"产品炮弹"，那向Anthropic投资50亿美元就是"战略绑定"。

这笔交易的结构很有意思：AMD不是简单地卖芯片给Anthropic，而是直接投资50亿美元，同时Anthropic承诺从2027年起采购最多2GW的AMD GPU集群。2GW是什么概念？一个中型核电站的装机容量大约是1000MW，2GW就是两个中型核电站的全部电力输出。按照目前单GPU功耗约1kW估算，2GW大约对应200万张高端GPU——这是一个天文数字级别的订单。

AMD为什么要自己掏钱投资客户？因为这不是一笔普通的买卖，这是"生态战争"。NVIDIA之所以能垄断AI算力市场，靠的不只是芯片快，靠的是CUDA软件生态——十几年来积累的数百万开发者、几万种优化过的AI框架和库、成熟的工具链。要撼动NVIDIA的地位，光有快芯片是不够的，你必须有大客户愿意陪你一起打磨软件栈、一起踩坑、一起把整个生态做起来。

Anthropic就是AMD选中的那个"生态锚点客户"。

看看Anthropic最近的动作你就明白了：5月签SpaceX Colossus数据中心，6月拿Google 5年5GW算力承诺，7月拿TeraWulf 20年190亿美元租约，现在又拿AMD 50亿美元投资和2GW采购协议。Anthropic同时在和Google、Amazon、SpaceX、TeraWulf、AMD五家供应商签约，分散在不同的技术路线、不同的地理位置、不同的电力来源。这不是浪费钱，这是算力层面的"不要把鸡蛋放在一个篮子里"——更重要的是，Anthropic在用自己的体量给AMD背书：连Claude背后的公司都大规模用AMD芯片了，其他AI公司还有什么好犹豫的？

对AMD来说，这笔账算得过来。50亿美元投资换来了什么？换来了一个全球Top 3的AI公司作为"旗舰客户"，换来了2GW的超级订单，换来了向整个市场证明"AMD芯片真的能跑最前沿的大模型"的机会。如果Helios在Anthropic的数据中心跑顺了，其他客户自然会跟进。这就像当年AWS率先采用AMD EPYC服务器芯片之后，整个云计算行业都开始大规模采购AMD一样——旗舰客户的示范效应是无价的。

客户名单里为什么有OpenAI？

Helios的客户名单里有一个名字特别耐人寻味：OpenAI。

OpenAI不是微软的"亲儿子"吗？不是和NVIDIA走得最近吗？怎么也出现在AMD的客户名单里？答案很简单：在算力这个问题上，没有永恒的盟友，只有永恒的需求。OpenAI今年的算力需求增长是爆炸式的——GPT-5.6、Mythos、Fable系列模型的训练和推理需要海量算力，微软一家的供应已经不够了。更重要的是，OpenAI比任何公司都清楚被单一供应商卡脖子的风险。

Meta也是Helios的客户。Meta今年在AI基础设施上承诺投入超过1800亿美元，但自己的模型业务变现一直不温不火，最近甚至开始"卖过剩算力"赚钱。既然自己用不完，顺便采购一点AMD的系统试试水、给NVIDIA一点价格压力，何乐而不为？Oracle更是企业云市场里"AMD的铁杆盟友"，一直用AMD芯片做差异化竞争。

这张客户名单传递出一个清晰的信号：AI行业对"NVIDIA之外的第二选择"的需求，已经压抑太久了。过去三年，AI公司不是不想用AMD，而是AMD的产品确实不够能打——软件栈不成熟、性能有差距、大客户验证案例少。现在Helios来了，性能对标Vera Rubin，有Anthropic、OpenAI、Meta这些头部客户的背书，"用AMD"不再是一个冒险的选择，而是一个合理的选择。

当然，AMD也不是没有挑战。NVIDIA的CUDA生态护城河不是一天建成的，把Claude、GPT这些大模型从CUDA迁移到AMD的ROCm软件栈上，需要大量的工程工作。但Lisa Su在发布会上明确表示，ROCm软件生态正在"快速成熟"，并且会对主流AI框架做全面优化。有Anthropic这种量级的客户深度合作，ROCm的迭代速度会非常快。

终局：1.4万亿美元市场的双雄争霸

Lisa Su在演讲中给出了一个大胆的预测：到2030年，AI加速器市场规模将达到1.4万亿美元——这接近今天整个半导体市场的规模。

1.4万亿美元是什么概念？全球智能手机市场一年大约5000亿美元，全球汽车市场一年大约2.8万亿美元。AI加速器在十年内要从几乎为零成长为和智能手机一个量级的市场，这个预测听起来激进，但考虑到Agentic AI带来的算力需求爆炸——每个Agent执行任务需要几十步推理、调用工具、处理数据——这个数字可能并不夸张。

在这个1.4万亿美元的市场里，AMD能分到多少？如果一切顺利，可能是30%-40%。这意味着AMD的年收入有可能从现在的几百亿美元增长到几千亿美元，彻底从"Intel的追赶者"变成"和NVIDIA平起平坐的算力双雄"。

但NVIDIA不会坐以待毙。Vera Rubin已经在发货，下一代Rubin Ultra也在研发中，NVIDIA的软件生态优势短期内仍然难以撼动。更重要的是，NVIDIA也在学习AMD——开始直接投资AI公司、签长期算力合约、和云服务商深度绑定。这场战争不是AMD单方面进攻，而是双方都在全力冲刺。

对整个AI行业来说，这是一件大好事。垄断意味着高价、意味着创新动力不足、意味着客户没有选择权。AMD强势崛起，会逼着NVIDIA加快迭代速度、降低价格、改善服务。最终受益的是所有需要算力的AI公司——以及每一个用AI产品的用户。

2024年你问AI公司买什么芯片，答案只有一个：NVIDIA。2026年你再问，答案会变成：看场景、看价格、看软件栈——NVIDIA和AMD都可以。从一家独大到双雄争霸，AI算力市场的格局，在2026年7月23日这一天，被永远改变了。

明天见。
cover.body_html_en:
$5 billion.

That's AMD's largest single investment in the company's history. On July 23 in San Francisco, at a packed Advancing AI conference, CEO Dr. Lisa Su laid two cards on the table. The first was Helios, a rack-scale AI system packing 72 Instinct MI455X accelerators, a 256-core 6th-gen EPYC Venice CPU, and Pensando Vulcano DPUs per cabinet — delivering 2.9 exaflops of compute, with multiple specs directly rivaling Nvidia's Vera Rubin. The second: a $5 billion investment in Anthropic, paired with a multi-year, tens-of-billions-of-dollars supply agreement under which Anthropic will deploy up to 2 gigawatts (2GW) of AMD Instinct MI450-series GPU clusters starting in H1 2027.

This wasn't a routine product launch. It was AMD's declaration of total war on Nvidia's monopoly in AI compute. When Lisa Su stated that "by 2030, the AI accelerator market is going to reach about $1.4 trillion — approaching the size of the entire semiconductor market today," you could feel it clearly: AMD is done playing second fiddle.

What Is Helios? A Rack That Is a Supercomputer

To understand Helios's significance, you need to understand what's happening in the AI compute market.

For years, AI companies bought GPUs "by the card" — thousands of H100s, tens of thousands of H200s — assembling servers, building networks, and tuning software stacks themselves. But as models grew larger and cluster sizes swelled from thousands to tens of thousands or even hundreds of thousands of cards, the "buy-by-card" model broke down. You could buy the GPUs, but that didn't mean you could wire them together efficiently to run large models. Network latency, cooling, power delivery, software optimization, fault tolerance — any weak link in the chain could drag real cluster utilization below 50%.

So Nvidia pioneered the rack-scale approach: from Grace Blackwell to Vera Rubin, packaging GPUs, CPUs, DPUs, networking, cooling, and power into plug-and-play "super units" that customers could simply power on and run. It was a ruthless strategy — shifting the competitive dimension from "whose GPU chip is faster" to "who can deliver a complete, out-of-the-box compute system." That play helped Nvidia capture over 80% of the AI training and inference market in two years.

AMD's Helios is a direct response to that strategy.

Helios's specs are aggressive: 72 Instinct MI455X GPU accelerators per cabinet, paired with a 256-core 6th-gen EPYC Venice CPU and Pensando Vulcano DPU, fully liquid-cooled, delivering 2.9 exaflops per rack. Benchmarks reported by The Register show Helios matching or beating Nvidia's Vera Rubin on several key metrics. This isn't a PowerPoint concept — Helios already boasts a star-studded customer list: OpenAI, Meta, Microsoft, Oracle, Anthropic, all with plans to deploy.

Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella publicly stated the day before the keynote that Azure would deploy Helios at scale for frontier model inference and Azure AI services. That's a landmark: Microsoft has historically been Nvidia's ironclad ally, with Azure's AI compute almost exclusively Nvidia GPUs. Now Microsoft is openly embracing AMD, sending an unambiguous signal: nobody wants to be locked into a single vendor.

"When you ask the agent to do something, it actually has dozens of steps — reason, call tools, access data, keep doing it over and over until it solves the problem — and so you need lots of GPUs to do all that."— Dr. Lisa Su, AMD CEO

The $5B Anthropic Bet: Not Selling Chips — Binding Ships

If Helios is AMD's "product artillery," the $5 billion Anthropic investment is "strategic binding."

The deal structure is interesting: AMD isn't simply selling chips to Anthropic — it's investing $5 billion directly, in exchange for Anthropic committing to purchase up to 2GW of AMD GPU clusters starting in 2027. What does 2GW mean? A mid-sized nuclear plant is roughly 1000MW; 2GW is the entire output of two mid-sized nuclear plants. At roughly 1kW per high-end GPU, that translates to around 2 million top-tier GPUs — an astronomically large order.

Why is AMD investing its own cash in a customer? Because this isn't a normal transaction — it's an ecosystem war. Nvidia's monopoly in AI compute isn't just about fast chips; it's about the CUDA software ecosystem — millions of developers, tens of thousands of optimized AI frameworks and libraries, mature tooling chains built over more than a decade. Challenging Nvidia requires more than fast silicon; you need anchor customers willing to grind through software stack tuning, debug alongside you, and co-build the ecosystem.

Anthropic is AMD's chosen "ecosystem anchor."

Look at Anthropic's recent moves and it clicks: May brought the SpaceX Colossus data center deal; June, Google's 5-year, 5GW compute commitment; July, TeraWulf's 20-year, $19 billion lease; now AMD's $5 billion investment and 2GW supply agreement. Anthropic is signing simultaneously with Google, Amazon, SpaceX, TeraWulf, and AMD — spread across different tech stacks, geographies, and power sources. This isn't waste — it's compute-level "don't put all your eggs in one basket." More importantly, Anthropic is using its scale to endorse AMD: if the company behind Claude is deploying AMD chips at scale, what other AI company has reason to hesitate?

For AMD, the math works. What does $5 billion buy? A top-three AI company as a flagship customer, a 2GW megadeal, and market proof that AMD silicon can genuinely run the most frontier LLMs. If Helios runs smoothly in Anthropic's data centers, other customers will follow. It's the same playbook as when AWS first adopted AMD EPYC server chips — after that, the entire cloud industry piled in. Flagship customer validation is priceless.

Why Is OpenAI on the Customer List?

One name on Helios's customer list is particularly intriguing: OpenAI.

Isn't OpenAI Microsoft's "protege"? Isn't it closest to Nvidia? Why is it on AMD's list? The answer is simple: when it comes to compute, there are no permanent allies — only permanent demand. OpenAI's compute demand growth this year has been explosive. Training and inference for GPT-5.6, Mythos, and the Fable family requires massive amounts of silicon, and Microsoft alone can no longer supply it all. More importantly, OpenAI understands the risk of single-vendor lock-in better than anyone.

Meta is also a Helios customer. Meta has committed over $180 billion to AI infrastructure, but its model business has struggled to monetize, and it recently started selling excess compute. If you can't use it all yourself, buying some AMD systems to test the waters and apply price pressure on Nvidia is a no-brainer. Oracle has long been AMD's staunchest ally in enterprise cloud, using AMD silicon as a differentiator.

The customer list sends a clear signal: the AI industry's hunger for "a second option besides Nvidia" has been pent up for too long. For the past three years, it wasn't that AI companies didn't want to use AMD — it was that AMD's products weren't competitive enough: immature software stacks, performance gaps, few large-customer reference cases. Now Helios is here, performance rivals Vera Rubin, and it has endorsements from Anthropic, OpenAI, Meta. "Going AMD" is no longer a risky bet — it's a rational choice.

Of course, AMD faces challenges. Nvidia's CUDA moat wasn't built in a day, and migrating large models like Claude and GPT from CUDA to AMD's ROCm stack requires significant engineering. But Lisa Su emphasized at the keynote that ROCm is "maturing rapidly" with full optimization for mainstream AI frameworks. With a customer of Anthropic's scale collaborating deeply, ROCm's iteration pace will be blistering.

Endgame: A Two-Horse Race in a $1.4 Trillion Market

Lisa Su made a bold prediction in her keynote: by 2030, the AI accelerator market will hit $1.4 trillion — nearly the size of the entire semiconductor market today.

What does $1.4 trillion mean? The global smartphone market is roughly $500 billion a year; the global auto market is roughly $2.8 trillion. For AI accelerators to grow from near-zero to smartphone-scale in a decade sounds aggressive, but given the compute demand explosion from agentic AI — every agent executing a task requires dozens of inference steps, tool calls, data processing — the number may not be outlandish.

How much of that $1.4 trillion market can AMD capture? If things go well, perhaps 30–40%. That could take AMD's annual revenue from tens of billions today to hundreds of billions, transforming it from "Intel's chaser" to an equal player in a compute duopoly with Nvidia.

But Nvidia won't go quietly. Vera Rubin is already shipping; next-gen Rubin Ultra is in development; Nvidia's software ecosystem advantage remains hard to dislodge in the near term. Critically, Nvidia is also learning from AMD — making direct investments in AI companies, signing long-term compute deals, and binding itself tightly to cloud providers. This isn't a one-sided AMD offensive; both sides are sprinting full tilt.

For the broader AI industry, this is excellent news. Monopoly means high prices, weak innovation incentives, and no customer choice. AMD's rise forces Nvidia to accelerate iteration, cut prices, and improve service. The ultimate beneficiaries are every AI company buying compute — and every user of AI products.

In 2024, if you asked an AI company what chips they bought, there was only one answer: Nvidia. Ask in 2026, and the answer becomes: it depends on the workload, price, and software stack — both Nvidia and AMD work. From monopoly to two-horse race, the AI compute landscape was permanently changed on July 23, 2026.

See you tomorrow.

briefs.body_html:
"我认为世界正在改变。我们现在所熟知的数学职业……我不认为它还会以现在这个样子存在。"

说这句话的时候，38岁的Jacob Tsimerman刚刚接过菲尔兹奖奖章。7月23日，2026年国际数学家大会在美国费城开幕，Tsimerman成为加拿大机构首位菲尔兹奖得主——也是当天最受瞩目的获奖者之一。记者会上有人问他未来打算，这位新科菲尔兹奖得主没有任何铺垫，直接摊牌：很快入职OpenAI，方向是AI安全。

没有过渡，没有"考虑一下"，领奖当天直接宣布加盟一家AI公司。这在十年前是不可想象的——菲尔兹奖得主的归宿通常是普林斯顿高等研究院、MIT数学系这种象牙塔顶端。但在2026年，连数学界最高荣誉的获得者都选择了直接跳进AI浪潮。

他是谁？为什么重要？

Tsimerman今年38岁，是多伦多大学数学系教授，因在o-minimality（o-极小性）和数论交叉领域的突破性工作获奖。他16岁上大学，24岁拿到博士学位，师从著名数学家Peter Sarnak，是当代数论和模型论领域最顶尖的学者之一。

更有意思的是，他的学生刚用Anthropic的Fable 5模型推翻了一个悬置87年的数学猜想。量子位在报道中提到了这一细节——这不是巧合。Tsimerman本人早已深度关注AI在数学研究中的应用，甚至可能已经在日常研究中使用大模型辅助证明。他对AI的理解，不是外行的好奇，而是一线研究者的亲身体验。

菲尔兹奖每四年颁发一次，授予40岁以下最杰出的数学家，被称为"数学界的诺贝尔奖"。历史上，菲尔兹奖得主几乎全部留在学术界终老。上一个有类似"跨界"举动的是Edward Witten——但他是从物理转向数学，还是在象牙塔内部。Tsimerman直接去了一家商业AI公司，而且是去做AI安全，不是去做AI for Science——这背后的信号比他个人的选择更重要。

顶级数学家为什么流向AI安全？

你可能会问：一个做o-极小性和数论的数学家，去AI公司能做什么？

答案是：AI安全恰恰需要最顶尖的数学头脑。当大模型越来越强、Agent越来越自主，一个核心问题变得越来越紧迫：我们如何从数学上证明一个AI系统是安全的？如何保证它不会在长时间运行中偏离目标？如何验证它的决策符合人类意图？这些问题本质上都是数学问题——涉及形式化验证、模型论、可解释性、博弈论、复杂系统等领域。

"当最聪明的数学头脑都开始认真对待AI安全，这说明这个问题的难度和重要性已经被严重低估了。"—— 一位AI安全研究员的评论

OpenAI不是第一次招数学家。过去两年，OpenAI已经从顶尖大学挖走了数十名数学和物理博士，超级对齐（Superalignment）团队虽然解散了，但AI安全研究反而在加速。Tsimerman的加入标志着一个新高度：连菲尔兹奖得主都来了。

这背后有两个趋势值得关注。第一，AI公司能提供的研究资源已经远超任何大学。在OpenAI，Tsimerman可以访问世界上最强大的计算集群、和最顶尖的AI研究者合作、看到最前沿的模型能力——这些是任何大学数学系都给不了的。第二，AI安全正在从"哲学讨论"变成"工程问题"，需要真正的数学工具来解决，而不只是写论文讨论伦理。

王虹、邓煜两位中国籍数学家也在本届菲尔兹奖获奖名单中——这是历史上首次有中国籍数学家获得菲尔兹奖。他们的选择同样值得关注。Tsimerman去了OpenAI，其他顶级数学家会去哪？Google DeepMind？Anthropic？还是继续留在象牙塔里？无论答案如何，有一点是确定的：AI正在虹吸整个科学界最聪明的头脑，数学只是开始。

明天见。
briefs.body_html_en:
"I think the world is changing. The profession of mathematics as we know it… I don't think it will exist in its current form."

Thirty-eight-year-old Jacob Tsimerman had just received his Fields Medal when he said those words. On July 23, at the opening of the 2026 International Congress of Mathematicians in Philadelphia, Tsimerman became the first Fields Medalist from a Canadian institution — and one of the most closely watched laureates of the year. When a reporter asked about his plans at the press conference, the newly minted medalist didn't hedge. He came right out with it: he would be joining OpenAI soon, to work on AI safety.

No transition period, no "I'll think about it" — announcing a move to an AI company on the very day he received the highest honor in mathematics. A decade ago, this would have been unthinkable. Fields Medalists typically ended up at the Institute for Advanced Study in Princeton, MIT Math, or similar ivory-tower pinnacles. But in 2026, even winners of math's highest prize are jumping straight into the AI wave.

Who Is He, and Why Does It Matter?

Tsimerman, 38, is a professor at the University of Toronto, awarded the Fields for breakthrough work at the intersection of o-minimality and number theory. He started university at 16, earned his PhD at 24 under renowned mathematician Peter Sarnak, and is one of the leading scholars in contemporary number theory and model theory.

More interestingly: his student just used Anthropic's Fable 5 model to resolve an 87-year-old unsolved conjecture. QbitAI mentioned this detail in its coverage — and it's no coincidence. Tsimerman has been deeply engaged with AI applications in mathematical research for some time, possibly already using LLMs to assist with proofs in his daily work. His understanding of AI isn't amateur curiosity — it's hands-on experience from a frontline researcher.

The Fields Medal is awarded every four years to the most outstanding mathematicians under 40, often called the "Nobel Prize of mathematics." Historically, Fields Medalists have almost universally remained in academia for their entire careers. The last comparable "cross-over" was Edward Witten — but he moved from physics to mathematics, staying within the ivory tower. Tsimerman is going directly to a commercial AI company, and specifically to work on AI safety — not AI for science. The signal here matters more than the individual choice.

Why Are Top Mathematicians Flowing to AI Safety?

You might wonder: what can a mathematician who works on o-minimality and number theory actually do at an AI company?

The answer: AI safety is precisely the kind of problem that demands the sharpest mathematical minds. As LLMs grow more capable and agents become more autonomous, a central question grows urgent: how do we mathematically prove that an AI system is safe? How do we guarantee it won't drift from its objectives over long horizons? How do we verify its decisions align with human intent? These are fundamentally mathematical problems — spanning formal verification, model theory, interpretability, game theory, and complex systems.

"When the sharpest mathematical minds start taking AI safety seriously, it means the problem's difficulty and importance have been drastically underestimated."— An AI safety researcher's observation

This isn't the first time OpenAI has hired mathematicians. Over the past two years, OpenAI has poached dozens of math and physics PhDs from top universities; the Superalignment team may have been dissolved, but AI safety research has, if anything, accelerated. Tsimerman's arrival marks a new threshold: even a Fields Medalist is coming aboard.

Two trends underlie this. First, the research resources AI companies can offer now vastly outstrip any university's. At OpenAI, Tsimerman will have access to the world's most powerful compute clusters, collaboration with leading AI researchers, and exposure to the most advanced model capabilities — none of which any university math department can match. Second, AI safety is shifting from "philosophical debate" to "engineering problem," requiring genuine mathematical tools rather than ethical discussion papers.

Two Chinese mathematicians, Wang Hong and Deng Yu, also received Fields Medals this year — the first time Chinese nationals have won the prize. Their choices will be worth watching too. Tsimerman went to OpenAI. Where will other top mathematicians go? Google DeepMind? Anthropic? Or stay in the ivory tower? Whatever the answer, one thing is certain: AI is siphoning the brightest minds across the entire scientific world. Mathematics is just the beginning.

See you tomorrow.

briefs.body_html:
你所有的睡眠数据、心率记录、步数、卡路里消耗、甚至血液检查结果——都可以直接喂给ChatGPT了。

7月23日，OpenAI正式宣布Health in ChatGPT完成全量上线，覆盖Free、Go、Plus、Pro全部四个套餐，Web端和iOS端同步生效。所有美国18岁以上用户，授权后可以把Apple Health、MyFitnessPal、Function等健康应用数据，以及部分医疗机构的电子健康记录（EHR）接入ChatGPT，让AI基于你的真实健康数据给出个性化建议。

这是OpenAI从"通用聊天机器人"走向"个人健康助理"的关键一步。但上线同一天，一则诉讼新闻给这场发布蒙上了阴影：一位美国牧师起诉OpenAI，称ChatGPT给出的健康建议险些让他丧命。

它能做什么？不能做什么？

根据OpenAI官方介绍，接入健康数据后的ChatGPT可以做这些事：帮你分析睡眠趋势和心率变异性，结合运动和饮食数据给出改善建议；解读你的血液检查报告，用通俗语言解释各项指标意味着什么；基于你的用药历史和健康目标，提醒你注意药物相互作用；甚至在连接医疗记录后，帮你整理就诊历史、为下一次看医生准备问题清单。

OpenAI反复强调：ChatGPT Health不是医疗设备，不能诊断疾病，不能替代医生。界面上会有醒目的免责声明，建议用户在做出医疗决定前咨询专业医生。但问题是——当一个AI工具能看到你所有的健康数据、能给你具体的健康建议时，普通用户真的能分清"这只是健康建议"和"这是医疗诊断"的边界吗？

这正是那起诉讼的核心争议点。据爱范儿报道，一位牧师声称他按照ChatGPT给出的建议调整用药后出现严重健康问题，险些致命，因此起诉OpenAI。OpenAI尚未对此诉讼做出正式回应，但可以预见的是：这不会是最后一起围绕AI健康建议的诉讼。

AI医疗的商业化与责任困境

Health in ChatGPT的上线，标志着AI医疗从"概念验证"进入"大规模商用"阶段。美国医疗市场规模超过4万亿美元，数字健康是其中增长最快的细分领域之一。OpenAI选择健康作为第一个深度垂直领域，商业逻辑很清晰：健康是每个人的刚需，用户粘性高，付费意愿强，数据价值大。

但健康也是监管最严、责任最重的领域。如果你让ChatGPT帮你写邮件写错了，大不了重写；如果你让ChatGPT给你健康建议出错了，可能出人命。这就是为什么OpenAI虽然功能上线了，但在措辞上异常谨慎——反复强调"不是医疗建议""不能替代医生"，本质上是在法律层面给自己留退路。

"当AI能看到你从心跳到血液检查的全部健康数据，它给你的建议就不再是'仅供参考'——用户会本能地信任它。"—— 一位数字健康领域投资人

从产品角度看，ChatGPT Health的想象空间很大。如果未来能接入更多数据源——比如连续血糖监测仪（CGM）数据、智能手表的心电图、甚至基因数据——ChatGPT可以成为一个真正的"全天候健康管家"。Google和Apple在健康领域布局多年，但没有一家拥有OpenAI这样的自然语言交互能力。你不需要打开五个健康APP看图表，直接问ChatGPT"我最近睡眠为什么变差了"，它就能给你综合分析。

但这条路不会好走。HIPAA合规、医疗责任界定、数据隐私保护、误诊风险——每一道坎都是硬仗。更关键的是用户信任：一次严重的误诊事故，就可能毁掉整个产品的口碑。

OpenAI选择在这个时间点全量上线Health功能，既是商业化的需要，也是在抢市场窗口期。Google Gemini、Anthropic Claude都在虎视眈眈健康赛道，先下手为强。但AI医疗的战争，最终拼的不是谁的模型更聪明，而是谁能在安全和责任的边界上走得更稳。

明天见。
briefs.body_html_en:
All your sleep data, heart rate records, steps, calories burned — even blood test results — can now be fed directly to ChatGPT.

On July 23, OpenAI officially announced that Health in ChatGPT had launched to all users, across Free, Go, Plus, and Pro tiers, on both web and iOS. All U.S. users 18 and up can, after granting authorization, connect health data from Apple Health, MyFitnessPal, Function, and electronic health records (EHR) from select medical providers — letting ChatGPT deliver personalized advice based on your real health data.

It's a critical step in OpenAI's evolution from "general chatbot" to "personal health assistant." But on the very day of the launch, a lawsuit cast a shadow over the announcement: an American pastor is suing OpenAI, claiming that ChatGPT's health advice nearly killed him.

What It Can Do — and What It Can't

According to OpenAI's official materials, ChatGPT Health can do the following once connected to health data: analyze sleep trends and heart rate variability, and suggest improvements based on exercise and nutrition data; interpret blood test reports and explain what each metric means in plain language; flag potential drug interactions based on your medication history and health goals; even, after connecting medical records, help you organize visit history and prepare question lists for your next doctor's appointment.

OpenAI is emphatic: ChatGPT Health is not a medical device, cannot diagnose disease, and is not a substitute for a doctor. Prominent disclaimers appear in the interface, advising users to consult a healthcare professional before making medical decisions. But here's the problem: when an AI tool can see all your health data and give you specific health recommendations, will ordinary users really be able to draw the line between "that's just wellness advice" and "that's a medical diagnosis"?

That tension is precisely at the heart of the lawsuit. As reported by ifanr, a pastor claims he adjusted his medication based on ChatGPT's advice, suffered severe health consequences that nearly killed him, and is now suing OpenAI. OpenAI has not formally responded to the suit, but one thing is predictable: this won't be the last lawsuit over AI health advice.

The Commercialization and Liability Dilemma of AI Healthcare

The launch of Health in ChatGPT marks AI healthcare's move from "proof of concept" to "mass commercialization." The U.S. healthcare market is worth over $4 trillion, with digital health as one of its fastest-growing segments. OpenAI's choice of health as its first deep vertical makes clear commercial sense: health is a universal necessity with high stickiness, strong willingness to pay, and massive data value.

But health is also the most heavily regulated, highest-liability domain. If ChatGPT writes a bad email, you rewrite it. If ChatGPT gives you bad health advice, you could die. That's why OpenAI, despite launching the feature, is being extraordinarily careful with its language — emphasizing repeatedly that it's "not medical advice" and "not a substitute for a doctor," essentially building legal cover for itself.

"When an AI can see everything from your heartbeat to your blood work, its advice stops feeling like 'for reference only' — users will instinctively trust it."— A digital health investor

From a product perspective, ChatGPT Health has enormous potential. If more data sources come online in the future — continuous glucose monitor (CGM) data, smartwatch ECGs, even genomic data — ChatGPT could become a genuine "24/7 health steward." Google and Apple have been laying groundwork in health for years, but neither has OpenAI's natural-language interaction capability. Instead of opening five health apps to look at charts, you can just ask ChatGPT, "Why has my sleep gotten worse lately?" and get a synthesized answer.

But the road ahead is bumpy. HIPAA compliance, medical liability frameworks, data privacy, misdiagnosis risk — each hurdle is a major battle. Most critically is user trust: one serious misdiagnosis incident could destroy the product's reputation.

OpenAI's choice to roll out Health broadly at this juncture is both a commercial necessity and a land-grab. Google Gemini and Anthropic Claude are both eyeing the health space, and first-mover advantage matters. But the AI healthcare war won't ultimately be won by whoever has the smartest model — it'll be won by whoever navigates the line between safety and liability most steadily.

See you tomorrow.

briefs.body_html:
做AI视频生成的开发者，过去一年最头疼的问题不是"没有模型可用"，而是"模型太多不知道用哪个"。

Runway Gen-4、Pika 3.0、Kling 3.0、Sora 3.0、Veo 2、Luma Dream Machine——每个模型都有自己擅长的风格和场景：有的写实人物强，有的运动镜头稳，有的生成速度快，有的价格便宜。开发者要么写一大堆if-else逻辑自己调度，要么硬着头皮只选一个模型凑合。7月23日，Runway给出了一个更聪明的答案：Media Router。

一句话概括Media Router：它是一个生成式AI领域的"模型调度员"——你只需要告诉它你要生成什么、优先考虑质量还是速度还是成本，它会自动帮你选择最合适的图像、视频或音频生成模型，把结果返回给你。开发者只需要对接一个API，不用再挨个集成十几个模型的API。

为什么模型路由会成为新基础设施？

Media Router的出现，标志着生成式AI市场进入了一个新阶段：模型层的竞争已经白热化，调度层正在成为新的瓶颈和新的机会。

想想大语言模型市场已经发生的事：一年前大家还在争论GPT-4和Claude 3谁更强，现在几乎所有严肃的AI应用都在用模型路由——简单问题用小模型省钱，复杂问题用大模型保证质量，特定任务用专门微调过的垂直模型。开源社区里OpenRouter、LiteLLM等模型路由工具已经成了标配，没有哪个AI产品会傻到只绑定一个大模型。

生成式媒体（图像、视频、音频）正在复制这个路径。2024年大家还在惊叹Sora的演示有多惊艳，2026年市场上已经有几十款能打的视频生成模型，各有优劣。Runway自己就是做视频生成模型起家的，现在它反过来做模型路由器，本身就是一个很有意思的信号：连模型厂商自己都承认，没有"一个模型打天下"这回事了。

Media Router的工作原理并不复杂：它在后端接入了Runway自研的模型，也接入了第三方模型（包括竞争对手的产品）。当一个请求进来时，路由器会分析prompt内容、用户指定的偏好（质量优先/速度优先/成本优先）、各模型当前的负载情况，自动路由到最优模型执行。如果一个模型生成失败，它会自动fallback到另一个模型重试——这对开发者来说是巨大的价值，可靠性一下子就上去了。

Runway的野心：从模型公司到平台公司

Runway这步棋，和当年Unity从游戏引擎公司转型为游戏开发平台有点像。

如果只做模型，Runway永远要和Pika、Kling、Sora们血战，拼模型效果、拼推理速度、拼价格，最后很可能陷入价格战利润微薄。但如果做路由层、做平台，Runway就变成了"生成式媒体领域的AWS"——不管你用哪家的模型，都要从我这里走，我收"过路费"。更关键的是，路由器掌握了所有请求数据：哪些prompt在什么场景下用什么模型效果最好、用户愿意为质量付多少钱、哪些模型在哪些任务上表现差——这些数据本身就是巨大的壁垒。

"当一个赛道有超过5个主流选择的时候，用户需要的就不再是第6个选项，而是一个帮你做选择的人。"—— 一位AI产品经理的评论

当然，Runway不是第一个想到做这件事的。开源社区已经有类似的多媒体路由工具，一些云服务商也在做多模型调度。但Runway是第一个有足够品牌影响力、有自研模型能力、有成熟开发者生态的玩家来正式做这件事。它作为"模型厂商"的身份反而成了优势——开发者会觉得："连Runway自己都觉得需要路由，那路由确实是刚需。"

生成式AI的下一波机会，可能不在"做出更好的模型"，而在"把现有模型用好"。模型能力每三个月就上一个台阶，但真正能把多模型能力整合到流畅工作流里的工具还很少。从Prompt工程到Agent编排，再到现在的模型路由，AI应用开发的基础设施正在一层层完善。

明天见。
briefs.body_html_en:
For developers building AI video generation, the biggest headache over the past year hasn't been "no models available" — it's been "too many models, no idea which one to use."

Runway Gen-4, Pika 3.0, Kling 3.0, Sora 3.0, Veo 2, Luma Dream Machine — every model has its own strengths: some do realistic humans well, others have stable motion, some generate faster, others are cheaper. Developers either write a mess of if-else logic to orchestrate themselves, or bite the bullet and pick one model to stick with. On July 23, Runway offered a smarter answer: the Media Router.

In one sentence: the Media Router is a "model dispatcher" for generative AI. You tell it what you want to generate and whether you prioritize quality, speed, or cost, and it automatically selects the best image, video, or audio generation model for the job, returning the result. Developers only need to integrate one API instead of stitching together a dozen model APIs individually.

Why Model Routing Becomes the New Infrastructure

The Media Router's arrival signals a new phase in generative AI: competition at the model layer is white-hot, and the orchestration layer is becoming the new bottleneck and the new opportunity.

Consider what already happened in the LLM market: a year ago people were still debating whether GPT-4 or Claude 3 was better; today nearly every serious AI application uses model routing — small models for simple questions to save money, large models for complex questions to guarantee quality, fine-tuned vertical models for specific tasks. Open-source tools like OpenRouter and LiteLLM have become standard; no AI product foolishly locks into a single LLM anymore.

Generative media — images, video, audio — is now following the same path. In 2024 everyone was marveling at Sora's demos; in 2026 there are dozens of capable video generation models on the market, each with tradeoffs. Runway built its name on video generation models; now it's building a model router. That in itself is a revealing signal: even model vendors acknowledge there's no such thing as "one model to rule them all."

The Media Router's mechanics aren't complicated: it connects to Runway's own models on the backend, as well as third-party models (including competitors). When a request comes in, the router analyzes the prompt, the user's stated preferences (quality-first / speed-first / cost-first), and current model load, then automatically routes to the optimal model. If one model fails, it automatically falls back to another and retries — which is enormous value for developers, immediately boosting reliability.

Runway's Ambition: From Model Company to Platform Company

Runway's move is reminiscent of Unity's pivot from game engine company to game development platform.

If it only makes models, Runway is forever in a bloodbath with Pika, Kling, and Sora — competing on output quality, inference speed, and price, likely ending up in a margin-crushing price war. But if it owns the routing layer, the platform, Runway becomes "AWS for generative media" — no matter whose model you use, you go through Runway, and Runway collects a toll. More critically, the router captures all request data: which prompts work best with which models in which scenarios, how much users are willing to pay for quality, which models underperform on which tasks — data that itself becomes a massive moat.

"When a category has more than five mainstream options, what users need isn't a sixth option — it's someone to help them choose."— An AI product manager's observation

Runway isn't the first to think of this, of course. Open-source multimedia routing tools exist, and some cloud providers offer multi-model orchestration. But Runway is the first player with enough brand clout, in-house model capability, and a mature developer ecosystem to formally ship this. Its identity as a "model company" becomes an advantage — developers think: "If even Runway itself thinks routing is necessary, routing must be real."

The next wave of generative AI opportunity may not be in "building better models" but in "using existing models well." Model capabilities leap forward every quarter, but tools that integrate multi-model capabilities into smooth workflows are still rare. From prompt engineering to agent orchestration, and now model routing, the infrastructure for AI application development is being built layer by layer.

See you tomorrow.

briefs.body_html:
三个哈佛大学辍学生，四年时间，做出了一家估值103亿美元的AI芯片公司。

7月23日，AI芯片初创公司Etched宣布完成3亿美元C轮融资，由红杉资本领投，a16z、SK海力士等跟投，投后估值达到103亿美元——距离上一轮50亿美元估值仅仅过去7个月，翻倍还多。更夸张的是，据知情人士透露，Etched已经在推进下一轮融资，估值目标是200亿美元。

这家公司的核心卖点很简单：我们做的不是GPU，是专门为AI推理设计的ASIC专用芯片。不需要GPU，就能跑任何AI模型，而且速度快很多。

为什么ASIC推理芯片现在火了？

要理解Etched为什么值100亿美元，你得先理解AI算力市场正在发生的一个结构性变化：训练和推理正在分化。

过去几年，所有人都在盯着训练算力——GPT-5训练用了多少张H100，Claude训练集群多大。但从2025年下半年开始，一个更明显的趋势浮现：推理算力需求正在爆炸式增长，而且增速远超训练。为什么？因为Agent来了。一个Agent执行一次任务可能要调用大模型几十次，推理token消耗量是传统聊天机器人的几十倍。当AI产品从"聊天"走向"干活"，推理算力需求就像打开了泄洪闸。

GPU是通用计算芯片，什么都能做，但什么都不是最优。训练大模型需要通用计算——因为模型架构一直在变，你需要芯片能灵活适应各种张量运算。但推理不一样，尤其是大规模部署的推理：模型架构相对固定，对延迟和吞吐量要求极高，这时候专用芯片（ASIC）的优势就体现出来了——砍掉所有不需要的电路，把所有晶体管都砸在矩阵乘法和内存访问上，能效比可以比GPU高好几倍。

这不是一个新概念。Google的TPU就是ASIC，AWS有Trainium和Inferentia，微软有Maia。但这些都是云厂商自研自用，不对外卖。Etched想做的是"第三方AI推理ASIC"——卖给所有需要大规模推理算力的公司，和NVIDIA的GPU正面竞争。

三个哈佛辍学生的"反共识"创业

Etched的故事很符合硅谷经典创业叙事。三位创始人都是哈佛的辍学生——2022年创立公司的时候，他们还没毕业。当时整个行业的共识是："AI芯片赛道已经挤爆了，NVIDIA不可战胜，创业公司做AI芯片必死无疑"。但这三个人不信邪，他们觉得GPU是为图形渲染设计的，不是为AI设计的，AI推理一定有专用芯片的机会。

"训练需要通用，推理需要专用——当推理算力需求占比超过70%的时候，专用芯片的时代就来了。"—— Etched联合创始人Robert Wachen

红杉领投这轮3亿美元融资，据说这是红杉历史上领投的C轮融资中估值最高的之一。a16z和SK海力士的跟投也很有信号意义——SK海力士是全球最大的HBM内存供应商，它的投资意味着Etched未来在高端内存供应上会有保障。

当然，质疑声音也不少。NVIDIA的CUDA生态护城河是所有AI芯片创业公司绕不过去的坎。就算你的芯片比GPU快5倍，如果主流AI框架不支持、开发者不愿意迁移、软件栈不稳定，客户还是不敢用。Etched声称其芯片能运行任何AI模型无需修改，但这一说法还需要大规模客户验证。

不过有一点是确定的：AI推理算力市场正在爆发，客户对NVIDIA之外的第二选择需求强烈。从AMD Helios到Etched的ASIC，从云厂商自研芯片到开源推理引擎优化，整个行业都在试图撼动NVIDIA在推理市场的地位。Etched能不能成功还不好说，但103亿美元估值说明：资本市场愿意为"NVIDIA替代者"这个故事买单。

毕竟，在一个1.4万亿美元的市场里，哪怕只分到5%的份额，也是700亿美元的生意。

明天见。
briefs.body_html_en:
Three Harvard dropouts. Four years. A $10.3 billion AI chip company.

On July 23, AI chip startup Etched announced $300 million in Series C funding led by Sequoia Capital, with participation from a16z and SK Hynix, at a post-money valuation of $10.3 billion — more than doubling from its $5 billion valuation just seven months prior. Even more striking: according to sources, Etched is already advancing its next round, targeting a $20 billion valuation.

The company's core pitch is simple: we don't make GPUs. We make ASICs purpose-built for AI inference. No GPUs required — any AI model runs, and runs several times faster.

Why ASIC Inference Chips Are Hot Now

To understand why Etched is worth $10 billion-plus, you need to understand a structural shift underway in the AI compute market: training and inference are diverging.

For years everyone fixated on training compute — how many H100s GPT-5 trained on, how big Claude's training cluster was. But starting in H2 2025, a clearer trend emerged: inference compute demand is exploding, growing far faster than training. Why? Agents. An agent executing a task might call an LLM dozens of times; inference token consumption is dozens of times higher than traditional chatbots. When AI products move from "chatting" to "working," inference demand opens like a floodgate.

GPUs are general-purpose compute chips — they can do anything, but aren't optimal for anything. Training LLMs requires general-purpose compute because model architectures keep changing and chips need flexibility to adapt to tensor operations. But inference, especially large-scale deployment, is different: model architectures are relatively stable, latency and throughput requirements are extreme, and that's where purpose-built chips (ASICs) shine — strip out all unnecessary circuitry, throw every transistor at matrix multiplication and memory access, and you can beat GPU energy efficiency by multiple times.

This isn't a new concept. Google's TPU is an ASIC; AWS has Trainium and Inferentia; Microsoft has Maia. But those are all cloud-vendor in-house chips, not sold externally. Etched wants to be the "third-party AI inference ASIC" — selling to any company that needs large-scale inference, going head-to-head with Nvidia GPUs.

Three Harvard Dropouts and the Contrarian Bet

Etched's story fits the classic Silicon Valley narrative. The three founders are all Harvard dropouts — they started the company in 2022 before graduating. The industry consensus at the time: "The AI chip space is oversaturated, Nvidia is unbeatable, startups building AI chips are dead on arrival." The three didn't buy it. They believed GPUs were designed for graphics rendering, not AI, and that purpose-built chips for AI inference were an inevitability.

"Training needs general-purpose; inference needs purpose-built — when inference demand crosses 70% of the total, the ASIC era arrives."— Robert Wachen, Etched co-founder

Sequoia led the $300 million round — reportedly one of the highest-valued Series C rounds Sequoia has ever led. a16z and SK Hynix's participation is also signaling: SK Hynix is the world's largest HBM memory supplier, meaning Etched will have supply security for high-end memory going forward.

Skeptics abound, of course. Nvidia's CUDA moat remains the wall every AI chip startup must scale. Even if your chip is 5x faster than a GPU, if mainstream AI frameworks don't support it, developers won't migrate, and the software stack is unstable, customers won't risk it. Etched claims its chips run any AI model without modification, but that claim needs large-scale customer validation.

One thing is certain: the AI inference market is exploding, and customers are hungry for alternatives to Nvidia. From AMD's Helios to Etched's ASICs, from cloud-vendor in-house chips to open-source inference engine optimizations, the entire industry is trying to loosen Nvidia's grip on inference. Whether Etched succeeds is an open question, but a $10.3 billion valuation shows: capital markets are willing to pay for the "Nvidia alternative" narrative.

After all, in a $1.4 trillion market, even capturing 5% is a $70 billion business.

See you tomorrow.

briefs.body_html:
Travis Kalanick又回来了。

这位Uber的联合创始人、以铁血和争议著称的创业者，在离开Uber之后沉寂了几年，终于拿出了他的下一个大赌注：工业AI和机器人。7月22日，据TechCrunch报道，Kalanick的神秘创业公司Atoms完成了一轮17亿美元的巨额融资，由a16z领投，Uber本身也参与了投资。

17亿美元是什么概念？这是今年具身智能/机器人赛道最大的单笔融资之一，比大多数机器人公司成立以来的累计融资都多。但奇怪的是，关于Atoms到底做什么，公开信息少得可怜。

17亿美元买一个"谜"？

TechCrunch在报道中描述Atoms的业务时用了相当模糊的措辞："用工业AI让世界现代化"（modernize the world using industrial AI）。公司的官方声明更是惜字如金，只说正在建设"下一代硬件和软件系统，以支持全球经济的自动化"。

没有产品演示，没有客户名单，没有技术细节，甚至连公司官网都只是一个简单的邮箱收集页面。你可能会问：这样一个"什么都不说"的公司，凭什么拿17亿美元？

答案很简单：因为它是Travis Kalanick做的。

不管你喜不喜欢Kalanick，他是硅谷过去二十年最成功也最具争议的创业者之一。Uber从一个黑车APP变成了全球出行巨头，虽然过程充满争议——漠视监管、与出租车行业战争、内部文化丑闻——但它确实彻底改变了城市交通。a16z愿意在几乎没有任何产品细节的情况下砸17亿美元，赌的不是Atoms的技术，赌的是Kalanick这个人——赌他能再次从零打造一个改变行业的巨型公司。

Uber参投这一点也很值得玩味。Uber现在的CEO是Dara Khosrowshahi，和Kalanick时代风格完全不同。Uber参投一家由创始人创办的机器人公司，释放的信号可能是：未来Uber的配送网络、自动驾驶车队，有可能和Atoms的机器人技术结合。毕竟Uber Eats已经在用机器人做外卖配送试点了。

工业AI为什么突然火了？

Atoms押注的"工业AI"赛道，正在从冷门变成热门。

过去两年，具身智能的聚光灯都打在人形机器人上——Tesla Optimus、Figure、宇树、波士顿动力，大家都在做长得像人的机器人。但一个越来越明显的事实是：人形机器人的商业化落地比预期慢很多，工厂、仓库、物流中心这些场景并不需要一个"长得像人"的机器人，它们需要的是能干活、成本低、可靠性高的专用自动化系统。

"消费级机器人的故事讲了五年还没赚到钱，资本开始把目光转向那些'看起来不性感但能赚钱'的工业场景。"—— 一位机器人行业投资人

这就是工业AI的机会。制造业、物流、建筑、农业、基础设施巡检——这些领域有大量重复性、危险性、高精度要求的工作，劳动力短缺问题日益严重。如果能用AI+机器人解决哪怕10%的场景，都是一个万亿级别的市场。Amazon的仓库机器人已经部署了几十万台，证明了这个商业模式是跑得通的。

Kalanick选择工业AI而不是人形机器人，可能正是看到了这一点。他是一个极度务实、对"赚钱"有执念的创业者——Uber之所以能赢Lyft，很大程度上是因为Kalanick对增长和效率的疯狂追求。他不会去做那种"看起来很酷但十年内赚不到钱"的人形机器人Demo，他要做的一定是能快速落地、快速规模化、快速产生收入的东西。

当然，17亿美元的估值对一个几乎没有公开信息的公司来说是疯狂的。上一个在信息这么少的情况下拿这么多钱的公司是OpenAI——但那是因为AGI的故事足够大。Atoms能不能讲好"工业AI"的故事，还需要时间验证。但可以确定的是：当Travis Kalanick拿着17亿美元All in一个赛道，这个赛道一定会变得更热闹。

明天见。
briefs.body_html_en:
Travis Kalanick is back.

The Uber co-founder, the notoriously ruthless and controversial entrepreneur, laid low for a few years after leaving Uber — and now he's placing his next big bet: industrial AI and robotics. On July 22, TechCrunch reported that Kalanick's secretive startup Atoms had raised a massive $1.7 billion round led by a16z, with Uber itself also participating.

What does $1.7 billion mean? It's one of the largest single rounds in embodied AI/robotics this year — more than most robotics companies have raised cumulatively in their entire existence. The odd part? Public information about what Atoms actually does is scarce to the point of mystery.

$1.7 Billion for a Black Box?

TechCrunch described Atoms's business in notably vague terms: "modernizing the world using industrial AI." The company's official statement was equally sparse, saying only that it is building "the next generation of hardware and software systems to support the automation of the global economy."

No product demos, no customer list, no technical details — even the company website is just a simple email-capture page. You might ask: how does a company that "says nothing" raise $1.7 billion?

The answer is simple: because Travis Kalanick is building it.

Love him or hate him, Kalanick is one of the most successful — and most controversial — entrepreneurs of the past two decades in Silicon Valley. Uber grew from a black-car app into a global mobility giant, and despite the controversy — flouting regulations, wars with the taxi industry, internal culture scandals — it fundamentally transformed urban transportation. a16z's willingness to pour $1.7 billion into a company with almost no public product details isn't a bet on Atoms's technology; it's a bet on Kalanick the person — a bet that he can once again build an industry-transforming giant from scratch.

Uber's participation is also intriguing. Uber's current CEO is Dara Khosrowshahi, whose style is a world away from the Kalanick era. Uber investing in its founder's robotics startup suggests that Uber's delivery networks and autonomous vehicle fleets could eventually integrate with Atoms's robotics technology. After all, Uber Eats is already running pilot programs for robotic food delivery.

Why Is Industrial AI Suddenly Hot?

The "industrial AI"赛道 Atoms is betting on is moving from niche to mainstream.

For the past two years, the embodied AI spotlight has been on humanoid robots — Tesla Optimus, Figure, Unitree, Boston Dynamics — everyone building robots that look like humans. But an increasingly obvious reality is setting in: humanoid commercialization is slower than expected, and factories, warehouses, and logistics centers don't actually need robots that "look human" — they need specialized automation systems that work reliably at low cost.

"Consumer robotics has been telling stories for five years without making money; capital is starting to look at industrial use cases that 'don't look sexy but print cash.'"— A robotics industry investor

That's the industrial AI opportunity. Manufacturing, logistics, construction, agriculture, infrastructure inspection — these domains are full of repetitive, dangerous, high-precision tasks, with worsening labor shortages. If AI + robotics can solve even 10% of these scenarios, it's a trillion-dollar market. Amazon has already deployed hundreds of thousands of warehouse robots, proving the business model works.

Kalanick's choice of industrial AI over humanoids may reflect exactly that insight. He's a fiercely pragmatic entrepreneur obsessed with "making money" — Uber beat Lyft in no small part because of Kalanick's manic focus on growth and efficiency. He's not going to build humanoid demos that "look cool but won't make money for a decade" — he's going to build something that lands, scales, and generates revenue fast.

Still, a $1.7 billion valuation for a company with almost no public information is wild. The last company to raise this much with this little disclosure was OpenAI — but the AGI narrative was big enough to justify it. Whether Atoms can deliver on the "industrial AI" story remains to be seen. But one thing is certain: when Travis Kalanick goes all-in on a sector with $1.7 billion, that sector is about to get a lot noisier.

See you tomorrow.

sources.text: 量子位 - 10万字速记一口吞，金山办公新Agent开始直接交活了

briefs.body_html:
10万字的会议速记，什么概念？按正常语速，大约是10-12小时的会议录音转写结果。正常人要把这堆素材整理成一份清晰的会议纪要，快的话要一整天，慢的话要两三天。

金山办公最新发布的WPS AI Agent说：给我，几分钟。

7月24日，量子位报道金山办公上线了新一代AI Agent能力——不再是以前那种"帮你写个邮件开头""给你列个大纲"的Copilot模式，而是直接处理长文档素材，输出可以直接交付的成品。10万字会议速记丢进去，几分钟后出来的是格式规范、逻辑清晰、有摘要有决议有行动项的会议纪要，甚至可以直接生成PPT版本给老板汇报。

从"辅助"到"交付"：办公AI的范式跃迁

过去两年，所有办公软件都在加AI功能，但大部分都是"Copilot模式"——AI给你打个下手，帮你补全句子、润色文字、翻译一下，最终决策和整合还是要你自己来。你让Word帮你写报告，它给你写个开头，后面还是得你自己写完。

WPS这次的新Agent，标志着办公AI进入了一个新阶段：从"辅助你写"变成"替你写完"。

这背后有几个关键能力的突破。第一是超长上下文处理——10万字相当于一本长篇小说的篇幅，能稳定处理这种长度的输入而不丢失关键信息，对模型的长上下文能力和信息提取能力是很大的考验。第二是结构化输出能力——不是给你一堆文字，而是按照会议纪要的标准格式输出，有明确的章节、重点标注、待办事项。第三是多格式交付——同一份内容可以输出为Word文档、PPT演示文稿、甚至思维导图。

量子位的报道里有一句话很到位："重做一遍WPS"。这不是在现有WPS里加一个AI按钮，而是从底层用Agent思维重构办公软件的工作流。以前的工作流是：人收集素材→人整理素材→人撰写草稿→人排版→人输出。Agent时代的工作流是：人丢素材→Agent处理→人审核→Agent输出。人的角色从"制作者"变成了"审核者"。

国产办公软件的Agent竞赛

金山办公不是唯——个在做办公Agent的，但它可能是目前在中文场景下做得最深入的。

微软Copilot当然很强，但它在中文长文档处理、中文会议场景理解、国内办公格式兼容性上始终有一层隔阂。飞书的AI能力也在快速迭代，但飞书更多是在协作平台层面做Agent，而不是在文档生产工具层面。WPS作为国内市场份额最大的办公软件，在中文文档处理上有二十多年的积累，现在把这些积累和大模型Agent能力结合，爆发力是很强的。

"以前评估AI写作工具看它'写得好不好'，以后要看它'能不能直接交活'。"—— 一位企业数字化负责人

这对内容创作者、行政人员、咨询顾问、分析师等"靠文档吃饭"的职业影响是巨大的。以前整理会议纪要、写行业报告、做竞品分析，可能要花60%的时间在"整理素材和搭框架"这种机械劳动上，真正的思考只占40%。Agent把那60%的机械劳动接过去之后，人的时间可以更多花在判断、思考、创意这些AI做不好的事情上。

当然，问题也存在。AI整理的会议纪要会不会漏掉关键信息？会不会错误总结某人的观点？生成的PPT是不是像那么回事？这些都需要时间验证。但方向是明确的：办公软件的AI能力，正在从"锦上添花的小功能"变成"不可或缺的生产力工具"。

当10万字速记几分钟就能变成可交付的成品时，你再花一整天去整理会议纪要，就不是"认真负责"，而是"不会用工具"了。

明天见。
briefs.body_html_en:
What does 100,000 words of meeting transcripts look like? At normal speaking pace, it's about 10–12 hours of recorded meeting time. For a normal person to turn that raw material into a clean set of meeting minutes — a full day if you're fast, two or three days if you're not.

Kingsoft WPS's newly released AI Agent says: hand it over, and you'll have it in minutes.

On July 24, QbitAI reported that Kingsoft Office had launched a new generation of AI Agent capabilities for WPS — no longer the old Copilot mode of "help you write an email opener" or "generate an outline," but the ability to process long document materials directly and output deliverable finished products. Feed it 100,000 words of raw meeting transcripts, and in minutes you get properly formatted, logically structured meeting minutes with summaries, resolutions, and action items — even a ready-to-present PowerPoint version for your boss.

From "Assisting" to "Delivering": A Paradigm Shift in Office AI

For the past two years, every office app has been adding AI features — but most have stayed in "Copilot mode": AI plays assistant, helping you autocomplete sentences, polish text, or translate, with final decisions and integration still on you. Ask Word to write a report, and it writes an intro — the rest is still on you.

WPS's new Agent marks a new phase in office AI: from "helping you write" to "writing it for you."

Several capability breakthroughs underpin this. First, long-context processing — 100,000 words is roughly novel-length, and reliably processing inputs at that scale without losing key information is a serious test of a model's long-context and information extraction capabilities. Second, structured output — not just a wall of text, but output formatted to meeting-minutes standards with clear sections, key-point highlights, and to-do items. Third, multi-format delivery — the same content can be output as a Word document, a PowerPoint deck, or even a mind map.

A line in the QbitAI piece hits the mark: "Rebuilding WPS from scratch." This isn't adding an AI button to existing WPS — it's reconstructing office workflows from the ground up with agentic thinking. The old workflow: human collects materials → human organizes materials → human drafts → human formats → human outputs. The agent-era workflow: human dumps materials → Agent processes → human reviews → Agent outputs. The human shifts from "maker" to "reviewer."

The Agent Race in Chinese Office Software

Kingsoft isn't the only company building office agents, but it may be the deepest in Chinese-language scenarios today.

Microsoft Copilot is certainly powerful, but it always hits a wall in Chinese long-document processing, Chinese meeting scenario understanding, and domestic office format compatibility. Feishu's AI capabilities are also iterating rapidly, but Feishu is building agents at the collaboration-platform layer rather than the document-production layer. WPS, as the office suite with the largest market share in China, has over two decades of accumulated expertise in Chinese document processing — combining that heritage with LLM agent capabilities creates real leverage.

"We used to evaluate AI writing tools on 'how well does it write'; from now on we evaluate on 'can it deliver the finished product.'"— An enterprise digitalization lead

The impact on content creators, administrative staff, consultants, analysts, and other "document workers" is enormous. Organizing meeting minutes, writing industry reports, doing competitive analysis used to mean 60% of your time spent on the mechanical work of "organizing materials and building frameworks," with only 40% on actual thinking. Agents taking over that 60% of mechanical labor frees up human time for judgment, thinking, and creativity — the things AI can't do well.

Questions remain, of course. Will AI-generated minutes miss key information? Will it mis-summarize someone's point? Will the auto-generated slides actually look presentable? Time will tell. But the direction is clear: office AI is evolving from a "nice-to-have feature" to an "indispensable productivity tool."

When 100,000 words of transcripts can become a deliverable in minutes, spending a full day organizing meeting minutes stops being "diligent" and starts being "not knowing how to use the tools."

See you tomorrow.

sources.text: TechCrunch - Meta launched a new AI optimism ad set to a song about human extinction

cao.body_html:
朋友们，今天这个AI圈乐子，够我笑到周末。

事情是这样的：Meta最近发了一条新广告，主题是"AI乐观主义"。画面拍得特别正能量——黑白镜头里一只眼睛在看关于AI抢工作、AI引发危机的恐慌头条，然后画面一转变成彩色，情侣在屋顶跳舞指着彩虹，青少年在湖里游泳，孩子在田野里奔跑，朋友久别重逢拥抱。旁白深情地说："有些人会让你相信AI会让我们更疏远，会把我们抛下。我们完全不同意。"

你猜这条广告的背景音乐是什么？是David Bowie的《Five Years》。

如果你对这首歌不熟悉，我给你讲讲它讲了什么。新闻传来，地球还有五年就要毁灭了，人类陷入恐慌。歌词开头是这样的："News had just come over / We had five years left to cry in / News guy wept and told us / Earth was really dying"（消息刚刚传来/我们还有五年可以哭泣/新闻主播哭着告诉我们/地球真的要死了）。

是的，你没有看错。Meta选了一首关于人类得知自己只剩五年寿命后集体恐慌的歌，来给"AI让世界更美好"的正能量广告当BGM。

小扎亲自转发，配文"未来属于所有人"

更绝的是，Mark Zuckerberg本人还在Facebook上转发了这条广告，配文写的是："Meta一直相信给人们分享、连接、按你想要的方式塑造世界的力量。当我们进入AI的下一波浪潮，我们继续相信未来属于所有人。"

朋友们，你们品，你们细品。"未来属于所有人"——BGM是"我们还有五年可活"。

TechCrunch的记者Amanda Silberling为了确认自己不是过度解读，专门找了她那个"平时不爱读书、喜欢Claude Code、做区块链分析"的弟弟问这首歌讲什么。她弟弟说："一开始以为是气候变化，然后以为是僵尸末日，然后以为是小行星撞地球。"是的，连一个不读诗的理工男都听出来了，这歌讲的是世界末日。

Meta的广告团队为什么会选这首歌？因为这首歌的副歌部分反复唱"people"（人们/人民），听起来很正能量、很振奋人心。如果不听前面的主歌、不看歌词、不知道这首歌的背景，你可能真的会觉得这是一首歌颂人类团结的歌。但只要稍微做一点功课——哪怕花30秒读一下歌词——都不可能犯这种错误。

这已经不是AI公司第一次广告翻车了

说出来你可能不信，Meta不是最近唯一一个广告拍出恐怖片效果的AI公司。

就在几周前，Anthropic发了一条广告，开头是一个着火的房子，然后切到一系列静态图片：被面部识别监控的人群、睡在街上的流浪汉、一排排墓地的墓碑、看起来像在矿场里挖矿的劳工。旁白是不同的人在问："AI能被信任吗？""如果需要刹车，谁来踩？"OpenAI的Sam Altman看完直接在X上吐槽："我以为这是讽刺账号发的，一直在找c1audeai之类的拼写错误。"

Palantir——这家给政府做AI监控系统的公司——名字来自《指环王》里黑魔王用来监视敌人的"真知晶球"（Palantír）。OpenAI CEO Sam Altman多次公开表示电影《Her》是他的灵感来源——而《Her》讲的是人类过度依赖AI情感陪伴导致的悲剧。Oculus当年给新员工发《头号玩家》——而《头号玩家》的背景设定是一个科技公司变得过于强大和邪恶的反乌托邦世界。

"科幻作家：我在书里发明了'折磨枢纽'作为警世寓言。科技公司：我们终于造出了经典科幻小说《别造折磨枢纽》里的折磨枢纽！"—— 一个经典Twitter梗

这些科技大佬们，一边从反乌托邦科幻里抄设定给公司和产品命名，一边拍广告告诉大家"AI让世界更美好"。这反差感，简直比Meta的BGM选择还要讽刺。

皮尤研究中心最近的民调显示，只有16%的美国人认为AI在未来20年会对社会产生正面影响，40%认为会有负面影响。看了Meta和Anthropic的广告之后，我好像有点理解为什么了——你连给广告选BGM都能选到世界末日主题歌，让大家怎么相信你们能把AGI管好？

给大家几个实用提醒

1. 做营销、做PPT、做发布会选BGM的时候，一定要完整读一遍歌词，不要只听副歌好听就用。Google一下"这首歌讲的是什么"只需要30秒，但翻车了要洗好久。

2. 发公关内容之前，找一个人文学科背景的朋友帮忙看一眼。科技公司可能觉得《Five Years》听起来很燃，但读过点书的人都知道这歌什么意思。雇一个英文系毕业生做营销审核，比出事后发道歉信便宜多了。

3. 如果你是Meta的市场部员工，今天可能不宜看Twitter/X。评论区已经笑成一片了。

今天就槽到这里，明天继续。
cao.body_html_en:
Friends, the AI industry has delivered a story so funny today that it'll carry me through the weekend.

Here's what happened: Meta recently released a new ad themed "AI optimism." The visuals are aggressively positive — a black-and-white shot of an eye reading panicked headlines about AI taking jobs and sparking crisis, then the world shifts to color: couples dancing on rooftops pointing at rainbows, teens swimming in lakes, kids frolicking in fields, friends embracing after time apart. A voiceover says earnestly: "Some people will have you believe AI is going to make us feel less connected. That it's going to leave us behind. We couldn't disagree more."

Guess what the soundtrack is? David Bowie's "Five Years."

If you don't know the song, let me tell you what it's about. News breaks that the earth has five years left before it dies, and humanity panics. The opening lines: "News had just come over / We had five years left to cry in / News guy wept and told us / Earth was really dying."

Yes, you read that correctly. Meta picked a song about humanity collectively panicking after learning it has five years to live as the BGM for its upbeat "AI makes the world better" ad.

Zuck Himself Shared It, Captioned "The Future Is for Everyone"

Even better: Mark Zuckerberg personally shared the ad on Facebook, writing: "Meta has always believed in giving people the power to share, connect, and shape your world in the ways you want. As we enter this next wave with AI, we continue to believe the future is for everyone."

Friends, let that sink in. "The future is for everyone" — soundtracked by "we've got five years left to cry."

TechCrunch reporter Amanda Silberling, to make sure she wasn't overreading, asked her brother — "a blockchain analyst who loves Claude Code and does not read for fun" — what the song was about. His answer: "I thought climate change at first, then zombie apocalypse, then an asteroid hitting the earth." Yep. Even a STEM bro who doesn't read poetry could tell: this song is about the apocalypse.

Why did Meta's ad team pick this song? Because the chorus repeats the word "people" over and over, sounding upbeat and inspiring. If you don't listen to the verses, don't read the lyrics, and don't know the song's context, you might actually think it's a tune about human unity. But with even 30 seconds of basic research — literally just reading the lyrics — this mistake was impossible to make.

This Isn't the First AI Company Ad Fail Lately

Believe it or not, Meta isn't the only AI company to release a horror-show ad recently.

A few weeks ago, Anthropic released an ad that opens with a burning house, then cuts to a series of still images: crowds surveilled by facial recognition, homeless people sleeping on the street, row after row of tombstones in a cemetery, what appear to be laborers toiling in a mine. The voiceover features different people asking: "Can AI be trusted?" "Who's gonna hit the brakes if we need to?" OpenAI's Sam Altman saw it and roasted it on X: "I thought this was satire, kept looking for the handle to be spelled c1audeai or something."

Palantir — the company that builds AI surveillance systems for governments — is named after the Palantíri, the "seeing stones" in Lord of the Rings that the Dark Lord uses to spy on his enemies. OpenAI CEO Sam Altman has repeatedly cited the movie "Her" as inspiration — "Her" being a film about the tragedy of over-reliance on AI for emotional companionship. Oculus used to give new employees copies of "Ready Player One" — a novel set in a dystopia where a tech company making VR products becomes overly powerful and evil.

"Sci-Fi Author: In my book I invented the Torment Nexus as a cautionary tale. Tech Company: At long last, we have created the Torment Nexus from classic sci-fi novel Don't Create The Torment Nexus!"— A classic Twitter meme by @AlexBlechman

These tech leaders lift names and concepts from dystopian sci-fi, then run ads telling everyone "AI is making the world a better place." The irony is thicker than Meta's BGM choice.

A recent Pew survey found that only 16% of Americans think AI will have a positive impact on society over the next 20 years, with 40% expecting a negative impact. After seeing Meta and Anthropic's ads, I'm starting to understand why — if you can't even pick a non-apocalyptic BGM for your ad, how is anyone supposed to trust you to manage AGI?

A Few Practical Reminders

1. When picking BGM for marketing, decks, or keynotes, always read the full lyrics. Don't just use a song because the chorus sounds catchy. Googling "what is this song about" takes 30 seconds; cleaning up after a PR disaster takes weeks.

2. Before publishing PR content, have a friend with a humanities background glance at it. Tech folks might hear "Five Years" as rousing; anyone who's read a book knows what it's about. Hiring one English major to review marketing copy is way cheaper than an apology tour.

3. If you work in Meta's marketing department, you might want to avoid Twitter/X today. The replies are already a roast session.

That's all the cao for today. See you tomorrow.
